coredata.dc:title: In situ synthesis of gold nanoparticles on N-doped graphene quantum dots for highly efficient catalytic degradation of nitrophenol
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Wenjuan
Ju, Yuyun
Chen, Yonglei
Chen, Xingguo
coredata.dc:description: In our work, a simple and green method of in situ synthesis of Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) on graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen (NGQDs) was reported. Construction of the nanocomposite was accomplished through mixing the NGQDs and HAuCl4·4H2O with no additional reductant and surfactant. The as-obtained Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposite exhibited high catalytic activity in its application in the degradation of p-nitrophenol (4-NP), a pertinacious pollutant occuring in industrial wastewater. Characterization results indicated that Au NPs were well distributed upon the surface of NGQDs and Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites were formed. Such Au NPs can provide high catalytic activity due to its naked catalytic surface with no surfactant capping. Turnover frequency (TOF) was used to examine the catalytic efficiency and its value was obtained to be 12 h−1. Furthermore, the as-obtained catalyst exhibited high reusability in the catalytic process of the degradation of 4-NP. This study indicates that such novel nanocomposite is promising for catalytic degradation of 4-NP.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au nanoparticles
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
Catalytic degradation
4-Nitrophenol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites [28]
Recycling ability of the Au NPs-NGQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (No. 21675068, No. 21705064).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image of the as-synthesized NGQDs.
TEM (A and B), HRTEM images (C) and XRD pattern (D) of the Au NPs-NGQDs.
XPS survey spectra of Au NPs-NGQDs (A). High-resolution XPS spectrum of Au4f (B), C1s (C), N1s (D) and O1s (E).

UV–vis absorption spectra of 4-NP solution (a) before and (b) after the addition of NaBH4, the inset shows the corresponding color of 4-NP solution. UV–vis absorption spectrum of degraded 4-NP solution(c).

UV–vis absorption spectra during the catalytic reduction of 4-NP by Au NPs-NGQDs (a). C t /C
0 versus reaction time for the reduction of 4-NP as a function of time with NaBH4 (b) (black line), with NGODs (red line) and Au NPs-NGQDs (green line), C 0 and C t was the concentration of 4-NP at initially and at time t (For interpretation of the references to color/colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article).
The C t /C 0 (triangle) and ln(C t /C 0) (square) versus reaction time for the reduction of 4-NP over Au NPs-NGQDs, respectively. C 0 and C t were the concentration of 4-NP initially and at time t.
Illustration of the proposed mechanism of the degradation of 4-NP to 4-AP by the Au NPs-NGQDs.
The conversion of 4-NP in nine successive cycles with Au NPs-NGQDs catalyst.
The TEM image of the Au NPs-NGQDs after nine cycles.

head.ce:title: In situ synthesis of gold nanoparticles on N-doped graphene quantum dots for highly efficient catalytic degradation of nitrophenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In our work, a simple and green method of in situ synthesis of Au nanoparticles (Au NPs) on graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen (NGQDs) was reported. Construction of the nanocomposite was accomplished through mixing the NGQDs and HAuCl4·4H2O with no additional reductant and surfactant. The as-obtained Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposite exhibited high catalytic activity in its application in the degradation of p-nitrophenol (4-NP), a pertinacious pollutant occuring in industrial wastewater. Characterization results indicated that Au NPs were well distributed upon the surface of NGQDs and Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites were formed. Such Au NPs can provide high catalytic activity due to its naked catalytic surface with no surfactant capping. Turnover frequency (TOF) was used to examine the catalytic efficiency and its value was obtained to be 12 h−1. Furthermore, the as-obtained catalyst exhibited high reusability in the catalytic process of the degradation of 4-NP. This study indicates that such novel nanocomposite is promising for catalytic degradation of 4-NP.
A green and easy-obtained Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposite was first used for the degradation of 4-nitrophenol and exhibited high catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:para: p-Nitrophenol (4-NP) is the by-product of the processes of manufacturing pharmaceuticals, pesticides and pigments in industry and regarded as one of the most venenous and hazardous pollutants [1,2]. It has been widely spread in the environment due to the wastewater discharged from industries. 4-NP can enter the food chain and cause irreversible damage to the organs of humans and animals [3]. 4-aminophenol (4-AP) has lower toxic and important applications in hair-dyeing agent, antipyretic drugs, and anticorrosion-lubricant [4]. Therefore, it is significant to change 4-NP to 4-AP. One of the notable methods to remove 4-NP is to convert 4-NP to 4-AP through catalytic reduction by borohydride ions (BH4−) with metal nanocatalysts [5]. Different kinds of metal nanocomposites have been synthesized and investigated due to their unusual physicochemical properties [6–11]. Compared with other metal catalysts, Au NPs are one of the most promising nanocatalysts due to their chemical stability and unique structure dependent optoelectronic properties [12,13].

However, the catalytic activity of Au NPs could be decreased due to the aggregation and minimization of their surface area caused by their high surface energy. The synthesization of metal nanoparticles on a stable substrate, such as carbon family, magnetic nanoparticles, polymers and metallic oxides provides a potential method to solve the problem [14]. For instance, Pd/CuO nanomaterial was synthesized and applied in the conversion of 4-NP [15]. Pd nanospheres was decorated on reduced graphene oxide and their catalytic activity was investigated [16]. Pd monometallic/bimetallic nanoparticles was immobilized on carbon nanotubes to catalyze the reduction of 4-NP [17]. Au@AuPt nanoparticles was embedded in B-doped graphene as an electrocatalyst for the determination of rutin [18]. An efficient and recyclable catalyst was also introduced by immobilizing Pd nanoparticles on magnetic fibers [19].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new member of the carbon family, with both the outstanding properties of quantum dots and graphene, are gaining enormous attraction because of their peculiar features like high accessible surface area, high chemical stability, less cytotoxicity, marvellous biocompatibility and outstanding water solubility [20]. The surface activities, electronic properties and local chemical characteristics of GQDs could be effectively modified by doping with heteroatoms (such as sulfur, boron, nitrogen atoms) [21–23]. Especially, graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen (NGQDs) can attune their electronic, physical and chemical properties resulting in remarkable features such as tunable luminescence, excellent biocompatibility and electrocatalytic activity [24–27].
Therefore, the synthesis of Au NPs on NGQDs substrate may exhibit excellent catalytic activity and accelerate the degradation of 4-NP to 4-AP mediated by NaBH4.

Hereinafter, in this work, the construction of the Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites was successfully attempted through in situ synthesis. This method was simple and facile, and did not need addition reductant and surfactant. The combination of Au NPs with NGQDs efficiently improved the catalytic capability of 4-NP conversion. Furthermore, the as-obtained Au NPs-NGQDs nanocatalyst could be reused for nine times and still show excellent stability because of the heteroatoms-doped composite structure.

Citric acid and dicyandiamide were bought from J&K Chemicals. (Beijing, China). Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O), sodium borohydride and 4-NP were obtained from Shanghai Sijun Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All the chemicals were analytically pure and used without purification. Ultrapure water was used throughout the experiments which was obtained through an AXLM 1820-V AXL water equipment (Aoyunda).

Hitachi-600 transmission electron microscope (Hitach, Japan) was used to record transmission electron micrographs (TEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX). Nicolet Nexus 670 Perkin-Elmer spectrometer was used to collected fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra. Agilent 5500 AFM was used to investigate atomic force microscopy (AFM) images. X-ray diffraction (XRD) were conducted by an X-ray diffractometer (X′ Pert Pro Philips, Cu Kα radiation, λ = 0.154,056 nm, 2θ = 5°–80°). Composition analysis of the Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites were proceeded on a Thermo Elemental IRIS Intrepid (USA) with a 4300 DV inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES). Absorption spectra was recorded by a TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (double beams, 1.0 cm quartz cell) (Purkine General Instrument Co. Ltd., Beijing, China). A PHI-5702 multifunctional spectrometer with Al Kα radiation was used to measure X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS).

NGQDs were synthesized according to reference [28], which utilize hydrothermal method to treat citric acid and dicyandiamide. Citric acid (2 g) and dicyandiamide (1 g) were mixed with ultrapure water (5 mL). The mixed reactants were dissolved completely by ultrasonic and then poured into a 25 mL autoclave lined with Teflon and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. When the reaction was completed, cooled down the reactor in the air to room temperature. The product with dark brown color was transferred to 100 mL ultrapure water and centrifugated for 10 min at 10 000 rpm. Then, the NGQDs were obtained by discharging the black residue and large dots on the bottom. The concentration of N-GQDs' aqueous solution was around 20 mg/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites [28]
ce:section.ce:para: Au NPs-NGQDs were prepared by refluxing: 2 mL 20 mg/mL synthesized NGQDs coupled with 2 mL ultrapure water were mixed in a dual-port flask (25 mL), then the aqueous solution was reacted at 100 °C for 10 min under stirring. After that, added 0.5 mL 0.1 M HAuCl4·4H2O and stirred the above solution for 20 min. Then Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites formed and the solution color also turned from yellow to black. Cooled down the solution to room temperature naturally and centrifuged for 10 min at 10 000 rpm. The product was dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

The conversion of 4-NP to 2-NP with NaBH4 was proceeded to investigate the catalytic property of Au NPs-NGQDs. First, ultrapure water (2.70 mL), 4-NP solution (10 mM, 20 μL), and fresh-prepared NaBH4 aqueous solution (0.1 M, 160 μL) were added into a standard quartz cell, respectively. Then, Au NPs-N-GQDs solution (0.4 mg mL−1, 120 μL) was added into the same quartz cell. The reduction reaction was then recorded at room temperature by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (250–500 nm). The rate constants of the reduction reaction were calculated by measuring the time-dependent changes in absorbance of the solutions at 400 nm.

The composition of the synthesized Au NPs-NGQDs was then analyzed by XPS. The spectrum clearly showed the existence of C, N and O. The three dominant peaks at 284.0, 400.0 and 532.0 eV should be possessed by C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The peaks at 84.0 and 87.8 eV could be assigned to the binding energies of Au4f7/2 and Au4f5/2. Fig. 3 B exhibited the high-resolution XPS of Au 4f, which signified the metallic state of the designed Au NPs adhered on the NGQDs. It should be mentioned that compared to the XPS peak of bulk metallic gold at 87.4 eV, the XPS peak of Au 4f shown in Fig. 3B displays a shift to 87.8 eV, which attributed to the interaction between Au atom and NGODs substrate [31,32]. Fig. 3C, D and E were the high-resolution XPS of C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The XPS spectra apparently demonstrated the well-doped nitrogen atoms in the framework of GQDs and the gold nanoparticles well-attached on the surface of NGQDs.

To evaluate the capability of the prepared Au NPs-NGQDs as nanocatalyst, the catalytic ability of the material was examined through the degradation of 4-NP. As displayed in Fig. 4 , in neutral or acidic conditions, the absorption peak of 4-NP was at 317 nm. With the adding of NaBH4, 4-nitrophenolate ion formed and the absorption peak at 317 nm changed to 400 nm instantly, accompanied with the color changing from light yellow to bright yellow. As seen in Fig. 5 a, the adding of Au NPs-NGQDs led to the color diminishment of 4-nitrophenolate and the solution became colorless within 5 min, simultaneously, the peak at 300 nm appeared, which belongs to 4-AP. It demonstrated the successful degradation of 4-NP to 4-AP. However, without Au NPs-NGQDs, the absorption at 400 nm kept unchanged along time even when excess NaBH4 was added in. It confirmed that the degradation did not occur without the Au NPs-NGQDs nanocatalyst. Same quantity of pure NGQDs was also used to evaluate their catalytic activity. As shown in Fig. 5b, the absorbance at 400 nm almost remained the same for 20 min, indicating that NGQDs have no catalytic ability for the degradation of 4-NP.

As the amounts of NaBH4 was excessive (C 4−NP:C NaBH4 = 1:80), its concentration would remain constant in the process of 4-NP degradation, the reaction kinetics could be regarded as pseudo-first-order kinetics [33]. Additionally, the 4-NP absorbance was in proportion to its concentration. Therefore, the kinetic constant k app can be represented as follow:
d
C t
d t
=
d
A t
d t
= −
k
app
t
or
ln
C t
C 0
= ln
A t
A 0
= −
k
app
t
where C t was 4-NP concentration at time t and k app can be determined from the plot of ln (C
t
/C
0 ) versus the reaction time t. As seen from Fig. 6 , ln (C
t /C
0 ) had a linear relationship with the reaction time t, which means the reaction kinetics of 4-NP degradation could be regarded as first-order kinetics. And k app was calculated as 1.17 × 10−2 s−1. The turnover frequency (TOF) is another critical parameter representing the ability of the catalyst. The definition of TOF is moles (or numbers) of the product molecules generated per moles (or numbers) of catalyst surface atoms [34,35]. The TOF of the Au NPs-NGQDs catalyzed degradation of 4-NP was 12 h−1.

The catalytic results showed the excellent catalytic activity of the Au NPs-NGQDs on degradation of 4-NP. The possible catalytic mechanism was proposed as follow: First, the as-obtained Au NPs with no surfactant involved offered a bare catalytic surface, which provides them with high catalytic activity [36,37]. Second, the speed of electrons transferred from
BH
4 −
to 4-NP using the bare Au NPs as catalyst could be enhanced by their NGQDs substrate [38], which could accelerate the electron uptake by 4-nitrophenolate ions and the subsequent reduction reaction. Third, as shown in Fig. 7 , like the graphene nano-sheet, NGODs presented a large ring structure, as the substrate of the as-obtained nanocomposites, which could help attract 4-NP through π-π stacking and thus enhance the concentration of 4-NP around the NGODs substrate. Therefore, the distance between 4-NP and Au NPs catalyst immobilized on NGODs substrate was shortened, which resulted in more effective contact between 4-NP and Au NPs. Fourth, with NGODs as stable substrate, Au NPs catalyst could well disperse on the surface of the nanocomposites and produce highly efficient contact with the reactants, and hence, demonstrated the high catalytic activity of the nanocomposites. These four factors contribute to the excellent catalytic activity of the Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recycling ability of the Au NPs-NGQDs

ce:section.ce:para: In this paper, Au nanoparticles on graphene quantum dots doped with nitrogen (Au NPs-NGQDs) were synthesized by a simple and low-cost in situ growth method. All kinds of characteristic and analytical results displayed that the synthesis of Au NPs-NGQDs nanocatalyst was successfully achieved. The as-obtained Au NPs with no surfactant involved offered a bare catalytic surface which provides them with high catalytic activity. With the nanocatalyst, the degradation of 4-nitrophenol could be achieved within 5 min and with more than 90% degradation efficiency. Furthermore, the nanocatalyst has good recycling ability for nine cycles with little catalytic loss. Therefore, this Au NPs-NGQDs nanocomposites hopefully would show wide potential applications in environment, catalysis and new energy fields.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (No. 21675068, No. 21705064).
